There’s two reasons 

number one the main animal that they raise as shepherds does become male after breaking their legs

Number two Sometimes people get osteoporosis when changing from female to male, and this can lead to them breaking their leg before the transition is obvious, but bone strength returns after they become male fully

I mean I am trans but not really. I honestly find superstitions to be super cool and the evolution of superstitions is very interesting to me.

They don’t mindlessly think breaking your leg makes you turn male there’s actually a lot of logic to it, it’s just wrong.
The country folk actually all things considered live pretty decent lives. The tech level is pretty high and they have very good medicine.
Lots of historical oppression, but doctors are uniquely trusted with very little association with these things.

Yeah pretty big divide. Urbanites are pretty distrusted by the country folk unless they are doctors or astronomers/astrologers.

The culture in a modern setting is actually more male dominated demographically. Historically it was overwhelmingly female but medical advances resulted in people living to 100+ fairly often

Females are pretty much all much stronger than males.

Country bumpkins often live in “harems” with a system where the eldest male is supposed to be impregnating all the other members who are female, and when he dies the eldest female becomes male.

This is the traditional way things worked evolutionarily but it just doesn’t work in most people in modern settings due to pheromone overload and people just change sex at 40.
And this strongly links to primogeniture too

I tried to do the history. but gave up on it. But suffice to say it used to be extremely irregular but the language has very aggressive folk etymology and regularization in its history.
There is in practice three types of verbs because active-stative distinction is present in intransitive verbs

Aelaki is a conlang that uses a logographic system and a triconsonantal root system or tetraconsonantal root system.

It has three grammatical genders: child, female, male, corresponding to the sequential hermaphroditism of the speaking species

The species start out their lives as female and become male at a certain point in their age. Normally around age 40

It uses polypersonal agreement with triconsonantal roots for intransitive verbs and tetraconsonantal roots for transitive verbs. 

complex concepts are normally expressed as genitive constructs which can be relatively complex. Because the grammar is quite rigid with its roots.

My idea is that that the alphabet developed from a syllabary like kana, where many of the older syllables came to be only used for their consonant value

About half the alphabet is syllables, and while these are on average used more than non-syllables, part of the letter is almost always reduced into a letter rather than a syllable
So a sizeable chunk of the letters are syllables, most of which have meanings as individual letters, but almost all complex words are written like an alphabet.
These are some of the most common “consonants” and there’s a similar presence of ya yu ye like in Cyrillic

Chinese, which is a really analytic language has tons of compound words

Because words are modified in a complex enough way that compound words would be complete nightmares. So genitive, adjective, and converb phrases tend to be used to express complex  ideas instead of compound words
My heavily agglutinative language has very few true compound words, often relying on genitive phrases instead
Yes exactly. It’s not agglutinative

ch-V-C-p is less common but still quite common. It comes from a verb that began with a vowel but had only one consonant like “up” or “aha” or “eep”
Almost all ch-i-C-p roots are dictionary words and if one isn’t then  you’d fool almost anyone by claiming it was
I forget the story behind -p but it’s a similar nebulous suffix

Yes.

Uchi- is a prefix in the proto language meaning to go do.

Because that’s a really nebulous meaning, it came to be the standard form of many verbs which were perceived as irregular when spoken in the short form. 

That actually transformed the other verbs too through folk etymology because ch- (often ch-i-) came to be seen as the default beginning

About 50% of verb roots begin with ch-

About 30% end with -p

I’m defining a verb root as meaning one that’s envowelled the majority of time as a verb. 

Nouns have a different behaviour. Because different affixes were added to lengthen them.
Longer roots are rare and usually compound words or loanwords. All monosyllabic words from the proto language had affixes added on that became parts of the roots.

Ch-i-r-p takes forms like

Chirap 
Ichirab 
Churup 
Churob 

So it’s not quite triconsonantal because there is a root vowel, but it’s affected by vowel harmony.

Contrast with ch-e-r-p

Cherap 
Echerab 
Chorup 
Chorob
Left to right top to bottom. I call them “triconsonantal” but the roots are really (CV)CVCC so the smallest and most common one has three consonants and one vowel.
Object is marked with the curl above which voices the last consonant and makes the second vowel into /i/
Kitop vs Kitib in a prototype of my abjad katakana. Subject and object form of the same noun
